title: Soy container candle -- jasmine
subtitle: A floral-scented soy wax candle in a glass jar with a clean single-wick burn
excerpt: Container soy wax at 8% fragrance load gives a good cold and hot throw in a 200 ml jar. The jasmine candle-grade fragrance oil is chosen over essential oil as it is more stable at pour temperature and holds its scent through a full burn.
sourceNotes: Soy wax fragrance load and throw: manufacturer guidelines for GW-464 container soy wax. Wick sizing for container diameter: candle supply industry standard CD series wick chart.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale the wax and fragrance proportionally. One pouring pitcher of wax (about 500 g) fills three to four 200 ml jars.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Candles benefit from a 48-hour cure time after setting -- the scent throw is noticeably stronger after curing.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Candles keep for two years at room temperature, away from direct sunlight. Discard if the wax surface turns greasy (fragrance bleed) or if the wick is too short to light.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hydrogenated soybean oil wax for container candles, melt point around 50°C. Holds 6 to 8% fragrance by weight. Sets to a creamy-white finish. Use container-grade soy, not pillar-grade, which is harder and gives a different finish.

content.text: Container candle making relies on two temperatures: the fragrance-add temperature (60°C) and the pour temperature (55°C). Adding fragrance too hot causes it to flash off; pouring too hot causes sinkholes.

is forgiving to work with and takes candle-grade fragrance oil at up to 10% of wax weight. A jasmine fragrance oil is used here rather than jasmine absolute, which is prohibitively expensive and performs poorly in wax.

items.prepNote: container blend; fills a 200 ml jar to 80%
jasmine-scented candle-grade fragrance oil (about 8.5% of wax weight)
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Press the wick sustainer tab to the centre of the clean, dry jar base. Hold the wick upright and lay a wick-centring bar (or two chopsticks held together with an elastic band) across the top of the jar to keep the wick straight during the pour.
Weigh 140 g of soy wax into the candle pouring pitcher. Melt over a double boiler, monitoring the temperature. Remove from the heat when the wax reaches 70 to 75°C and is fully clear and melted.

Let the wax cool to 60°C, then add 12 g of fragrance oil. Stir slowly and steadily for 30 seconds. When the wax has cooled to 55°C, pour steadily into the prepared jar, keeping the wick centred. Fill to about 1.5 cm below the rim.

Leave undisturbed at room temperature for at least two hours. Do not move the jar while the wax is setting. Once fully set, trim the wick to 5 mm. Leave the candle to cure for 48 hours before the first burn -- the fragrance throw improves significantly with curing time.

For the first burn, allow the wax to melt all the way to the edges of the jar -- this takes about 2 to 3 hours. This prevents tunnelling (a hollow forming around the wick that limits fragrance throw on subsequent burns). Trim the wick to 5 mm before every burn. Never leave a burning candle unattended.
